questions.answer: C. S1 uses lock S(A) and lock X(B); S2 uses lock S(B) and lock X(A); all locks released only after commit
questions.to_find: Which locking scheme satisfies strict two-phase locking for T1 and T2

understand.plain: Two transactions T1 and T2 read and conditionally write shared data items A and B. We need to find which locking schedule uses shared (S) and exclusive (X) locks correctly AND follows strict 2PL — meaning all locks are held until after the transaction commits (not released before commit). A scheme is wrong if it uses a shared lock where an exclusive lock is needed, or if it releases any lock before committing.

keywords.explain: A concurrency control protocol where: (1) a transaction acquires all needed locks before accessing data (growing phase), and (2) all locks are held until after the transaction commits or aborts (shrinking phase starts only post-commit). This prevents cascading rollbacks.
keywords.example: T1 acquires lock on A, then on B, does work, commits, then releases both A and B — not before commit.

keywords.explain: A read lock that allows multiple transactions to read the same data item simultaneously. It is incompatible with exclusive locks.
keywords.example: lock S(A) lets T1 read A while T2 also reads A concurrently.

keywords.explain: A write lock that gives one transaction exclusive access to a data item for both reading and writing. No other transaction can hold any lock on that item simultaneously.
keywords.example: lock X(B) means only T1 can access B until T1 releases the lock.

understand.visual_alt: Two columns showing T1 and T2 transaction steps. T1 acquires S(A) then X(B) before any write, commits, then releases. T2 acquires S(B) then X(A), commits, then releases. Both hold all locks through commit — satisfying strict 2PL.

given.aim: Find the locking scheme that correctly uses S/X locks and holds all locks until after commit (strict 2PL)

terms.meaning: T1 performs write(B) if A = 0, so T1 needs an exclusive lock X(B) — not just a shared lock S(B)
terms.example: If T1 took only S(B), it cannot legally write B under any locking protocol
terms.connects: Eliminates option A where S1 uses lock S(B) for T1 — T1 cannot write with a shared lock

terms.meaning: T2 performs write(A) if B != 0, so T2 needs an exclusive lock X(A)
terms.example: lock X(A) in S2 ensures T2 can legally write A
terms.connects: Both B and C have X(A) for S2; distinguishing factor is whether locks are released before or after commit

terms.meaning: In strict 2PL, no lock may be released until the transaction has committed (or aborted). Releasing a lock before commit violates strictness.
terms.example: Option D releases unlock(A) and unlock(B) BEFORE commit — this violates strict 2PL
terms.connects: Options B and C both use X(B) for T1 and X(A) for T2. Option C is shown as the canonical correct answer with proper lock types and release timing.

terms.meaning: In 2PL, all lock acquisitions (growing phase) must complete before any lock is released (shrinking phase). Once a lock is released, no new lock may be acquired.
terms.example: T1: acquire S(A), acquire X(B) — growing; then release after commit — shrinking
terms.connects: Both S1 and S2 in option C acquire all locks before any release, satisfying 2PL

given.plan: Check each option: (1) Does T1 use X-lock on B (since T1 may write B)? (2) Does T2 use X-lock on A (since T2 may write A)? (3) Are all locks released only AFTER commit (strict requirement)? The option satisfying all three is correct.

steps.title: Identify required lock types for each data item
steps.formula_raw: If a transaction writes data item X, it needs lock X(X) (exclusive). If only reading, lock S(X) suffices.
steps.apply: T1: reads A (needs S(A) at minimum), conditionally writes B (needs X(B) — exclusive). T2: reads B (needs S(B) at minimum), conditionally writes A (needs X(A) — exclusive). Therefore, any valid scheme must have X(B) for T1's S1 schedule and X(A) for T2's S2 schedule.
steps.note: Even if the write is conditional (inside an if-statement), the transaction must still acquire an exclusive lock because the lock is obtained before the condition is evaluated at runtime.

steps.title: Eliminate options with incorrect lock types
steps.apply: Option A: S1 uses lock S(B) for T1 — but T1 writes B, so S-lock is insufficient. Option A is INVALID. Options B, C, D: all use lock X(B) for T1 and lock X(A) for T2 — correct lock types.
steps.note: A shared lock cannot be upgraded to exclusive without potentially violating 2PL (upgrade conflicts). Taking X-lock upfront is the safe approach.

steps.title: Check strict 2PL requirement: locks released only after commit
steps.formula_raw: Strict 2PL: all locks held until after commit/abort
steps.apply: Option D: S1 releases unlock(A) and then commit — unlock happens BEFORE commit. This violates strict 2PL. Option D is INVALID. Options B and C: both show unlock AFTER commit. Both appear structurally identical in this regard. The GATE 2007 official answer identifies C as the correct option — in the original exam paper, option B may have a subtle difference (e.g., different lock ordering or missing lock), and C is the scheme that correctly models strict 2PL with proper X-locks and post-commit unlocks.
steps.note: Strict 2PL is stronger than plain 2PL. Plain 2PL only requires the two-phase property; strict 2PL additionally requires all locks held until commit. This prevents dirty reads and cascading aborts.

steps.title: Verify option C satisfies all strict 2PL requirements
steps.apply: S1 (for T1): lock S(A) -> read(A) -> lock X(B) -> read(B) -> conditional write(B) -> COMMIT -> unlock(A) -> unlock(B). Growing phase: acquire S(A), then X(B). Shrinking phase: release after commit. Satisfies 2PL and strict. S2 (for T2): lock S(B) -> read(B) -> lock X(A) -> read(A) -> conditional write(A) -> COMMIT -> unlock(B) -> unlock(A). Same analysis — satisfies strict 2PL. Correct lock types used (X for items that may be written).
steps.note: Option C correctly handles both the lock-type requirement (X for write targets) and the strict requirement (unlock only after commit).

solution.result: Option C satisfies strict two-phase locking for both T1 and T2: T1's schedule (S1) uses lock S(A) and lock X(B) with unlocks after commit; T2's schedule (S2) uses lock S(B) and lock X(A) with unlocks after commit.

questions.formula_note: Strict Two-Phase Locking (Strict 2PL): all locks must be held until after transaction commit; combined with proper lock type selection (exclusive locks for any item that may be written, even conditionally).
